First-Order Differential Equations                                                                                                   General form
                                                                                                                                       Definition 1
                                                                                                                                       An ordinary differential equation or ODE is an equation
    I A differential equation is an equation that relates an                                                                           involving one independent real variable (e.g., x ∈ R), one
      unknown function with one or more of its derivatives.                                                                            dependent real variable (e.g., u : R → R) and finitely many of
                                                                                                                                       its derivatives. Thus, an ODE can always be put in the form
    I When only ordinary derivatives, rather than partial
                                                                                                                                                                                         
      derivatives, are involved, the equations are called ordinary                                                                                    F x, u(x), u′ (x), . . . , u(n) (x) = 0.    (1)
      differential equations (ODEs).
    I Differential equations involving partial derivatives are
                                                                                                                                       Example 2
      called partial differential equations (PDEs). You will learn
      them in Weeks 9 and 10.                                                                                                          The following are examples of ODEs

                                                                                                                                                        u′ (x) = 2u(x) + 3x
                                                                                                                                                        u′′ (x) = u′ (x) + u2 (x) + x3

 Linear ODEs                                                                                                                    Solving ODEs
  Definition 5                                                                                                                     I Not all ODEs can be solved explicitly. In this course, you
  An ODE is said to be linear if the unknown function and all its                                                                    will learn methods to solve separable equations, linear
  derivatives appear linearly.                                                                                                       first-order and second-order equations.
                                                                                                                                   I General rules:
  Example 6
                                                                                                                                        1. The general solution to an ODE involves some constants
  Which equations in Example 4 are linear?
                                                                                                                                           of integration. It is crucial to keep a careful track of
                                                                                                                                           them.
                                                                                                                                        2. The number of constants of integration equals the order
                                                                                                                                           of the ODE.
                                                                                                                                        3. If an ODE is given with some initial conditions (ICs),
                                                                                                                                           the ICs are used to determine values for the constants of
                                                                                                                                           integration.
                                                                                                                                   I MATH2089 covers some numerical methods for solving
                                                                                                                                     computing approximate solutions to ODEs.

                                                                                                                              Homeogenous Second-Order ODEs
                                                                                                                               Definition 17
                                                                                                                               A homogeneous linear second-order ODE with constant
                                                                                                                               coeﬀicients has the form

                                                                                                                                                        y ′′ + ay ′ + by = 0                                                (HE)

                                                                                                                               where a and b are real constants.
                                                                                                                                I The general solution of any second order ODE must have
                                                                                                                                  two constants of integration, and hence two initial
                                                                                                                                  conditions are needed to determine these constants.

                                                                                                                                I Superposition of solutions is an important property of all
                                                                                                                                  linear homogeneous ODEs (of any order). It will be used
  3. 6.6 mg                                                                                                                       to solve linear second-order equations. In the following we
                                                                                                                                  only prove this property for second-order equations.
                                                 .   .   .     . . . .     . . . .     . . . .       .   .       .   .   .                                                         .   .   .     . . . .     . . . .     . . . .       .   .       .   .   .
                                             .   .   .       . . . .     . . . .     . . . .     .   .       .   .   .                                                         .   .   .       . . . .     . . . .     . . . .     .   .       .   .   .




Superposition of solutions                                                                                                     Proof.
                                                                                                                               We prove only (1). First we note that if y1 (t) and y2 (t) are
                                                                                                                               solutions then

                                                                                                                                        y1′′ + ay1′ + by1 = 0   and y2′′ + ay2′ + by2 = 0.
  Theorem 18
    1. If y1 and y2 are two solutions of (HE) then so is any linear                                                            If y = c1 y1 + c2 y2 then
       combination c1 y1 + c2 y2 , where c1 and c2 are constants.

    2. (HE) has two linearly independent solutions y1 and y2 .
       Moreover, every solution y is a linear combination of
       these solutions, i.e., there exist constants c1 and c2 such
       that y = c1 y1 + c2 y2 .

Solve each of the following second order ODEs:
 1. y ′′ − 5y ′ + 6y = 0
 2. y ′′ − 6y ′ + 9y = 0
 3. y ′′ − 6y ′ + 58y = 0.

                                                                                                                          Free oscillations

                                                                                                                             Consider the motion of a mass m > 0 attached to a spring so
                                                                                                                             that it can move back and forth in the horizontal direction.
                                                                                                                             Let y(t) be the horizontal displacement of the mass from its
                                                                                                                             equilibrium position (where the spring is at its natural length).
                                                                                                                             The velocity of the mass is ẏ = dy/dt and the acceleration is
(1) y = Ae2t + Be3t , (2) y = (A + Bt)e3t ,                                                                                  ÿ = d2 y/dt2 .
